narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as an uphill battle—78% chance of not passing, based on patterns from thousands of students like you. Picture your story through the model's eyes: it started with your Year 3 grades landing you in the bottom decile (1.0) at school, which stood out as the biggest red flag, whispering that the rigors of legal studies might overwhelm even with determination. That low decile overshadowed your Year 1 performance, where you were in the 3rd decile—solid start, but the model saw the sharp drop-off as the strongest signal of struggle ahead. Layer on your LSAT score of 22, which echoed that concern, pulling heavily toward the risk of falling short on the bar's high-stakes testing. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.2 added to the picture, suggesting foundational academics that might not fully support the marathon of law school demands. Coming from a middle-income family painted you as resourceful but without the extra cushions some peers have for tutoring or prep courses, nudging the outlook dimmer. As an Asian female going full-time, these traits aligned with profiles the model has seen falter more often under pressure, each one tilting the scales further toward failure. It's not about your spirit or potential—it's this specific mosaic of your grades dipping low, test scores trailing, and supports feeling just average that convinced the model you'd face tough odds. Many in similar spots have rallied with targeted boosts, like LSAT retakes or grade recovery plans, turning predictions around. You've got the target set on law; now, imagine rewriting this chapter with those edges sharpened. We're rooting for your comeback story.
